questions.prompt: Why does a GPU sometimes run a single small operation slower than a CPU even though the GPU has thousands of cores?

choices.text: Overhead and clock speed. Moving data CPU-to-GPU over PCIe costs microseconds; launching a CUDA kernel costs another ~5-10 microseconds; the GPU runs at ~1.5-2 GHz vs. a CPU's 4-5+ GHz; and individual GPU threads have no out-of-order execution or branch prediction. For a single small calculation, the CPU finishes before the GPU has even started. The GPU only wins when there's enough parallel work to amortize the overhead and keep its thousands of cores busy. This is why GPU speedup depends strongly on workload size — a 100x100 matmul might be slower on GPU, a 10,000x10,000 matmul is dramatically faster.

questions.explanation: GPU overhead is real and only pays off at scale. Knowing where the breakeven is for your specific workload matters more than 'GPU good, CPU bad' intuition.

questions.prompt: An LLM inference service is bottlenecked. The team confirms GPU compute utilization is only 30% but memory bandwidth utilization is 95%. What's the diagnosis?

choices.text: Memory-bandwidth-bound, specifically because the decode phase of LLM inference has low arithmetic intensity (FLOPs per byte). Each new token's generation requires reading the full set of model weights from memory but performs relatively little compute on them, so the GPU's compute units sit idle while waiting for memory. The fix isn't more compute — it's increasing arithmetic intensity. Options include: larger batch sizes (amortize weight reads across more queries), KV-caching (reuse attention computation), speculative decoding (draft model + verify), quantization (smaller weights = less memory traffic), or specialized kernels that reuse data in shared memory. Adding GPUs without changing the workload pattern wouldn't help because each GPU would be just as memory-bound.

questions.explanation: The roofline model in action. Memory-bound workloads don't benefit from more compute; they benefit from better data reuse and higher arithmetic intensity. This is why LLM inference optimization is mostly about batching, caching, and quantization — not about faster GPUs.
